replies.author: @carlrood4457
replies.text:
@michaelsaint9181 I think the problem is when you only had the people in your immediate vicinity to deal with, you had a sense that your hobbies, beliefs, etc. were mainstream or not.  You knew if you were on the fringe because you only met a very small percentage of people who shared them.  Now, you can hook up with thousands, if not millions online and you can ignore the mainstream.  Even though it's still just a tiny percentage of the world population, it feels like validation from a huge number.  More centered people are simply still living their lives, so their lack of engagement either seems like agreement or non-existence to such people.  As such they have not developed personal censors.

It may well be that the smartphone is the worst invention for a functional society.  Strangely enough, I'm reminded of an episode of Gilligan's Island.   They discover sunflower seeds that enable people to read minds when eaten.  Every stray thought starts getting taken as an insult and everyone is fighting.

replies.author: @carlrood4457
replies.text:
@L1A1Rocker Answer: Family.  Kurt's father was character actor, Bing Russell.  If you watch old movies and TV from the 50's and 60's, he's one of those guys you see in everything, but never know their name.  Kurt actually got his first acting job because a show on the next soundstage needed a kid.  Bing played Vernon Presley, when Kurt starred as Elvis.  A baseball fan, he went on to own a minor league team for a while.

I'd say being a working class actor probably gave him the insight to raise Kurt with realistic expectations about the business.  Kurt, himself, had something of a dry spell in the mid to late 70's as he transitioned from child/teen actor to adult. He even tried a hand playing minor league baseball.  You see it in him today as he's a devoted family man with Goldie Hawn and lives away from Hollywood.  He's basically a live and let live kind of guy.

replies.author: @lasourisaboyante
replies.text:
​@Aurabyss_01I haven't been in a street fight in my adult life.  But I did toughman contests and various martial arts tournaments in college and grad school.  Mainly to see how I'd do, which was pretty well.

Since then, just a handful of incidents.  None rise to the level of a fight, just a quick scuffle.  But I will categorically not let you up until the cops get there.  It is very, very dangerous.  No ref to stop it.  If they get the upper hand, you are at their mercy.  So I'm not going to allow that if I'm able.

Never been arrested so each was judged to be righteous and appropriate.  Also never really hurt anyone.  Worst was a broken arm.  And each time there were plenty of witnesses or cameras.

Last time was last year.  I guess as I age, I look like more of a mark.
